speaker: interviewer
text: 9Th of October, 1995, Henry Kinsley, tape 4. You were saying that you were put on transport out of Plaszow. The men and women were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Women were separated. There-- there we found out they went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The women. We went to Gross-Rosen. After three or four days of Gross-Rosen, they put us again on the cattle trains. And we wound up in Brunnlitz. The women stayed in Auschwitz, I don't know, a month, or something, or less. She will tell you-- Irene-- a month, because she went--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Gross-Rosen for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that you were going to be out of there? Or did you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew, because they treat us like we were in transit. I mean, they took away all our clothing, searched us, they gave us stripes, uniforms, and for-- they called us over there again, and put us on transport. We wind up after--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a number at any point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only on the uniform-- the number sewed in. But the women in Plaszow, they got it on the hands. And there was a rule over there-- anybody who left the camp outside for work had to wear a striped suit and a--and a number, in case he runs away, to recognize. We, in Madritsch, never left the camp. So we didn't have numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the camp Gross-Rosen different to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or they were a crazy house-- crazy. They took us the first day-- after we-- it was cold like hell. We had to drop the-- the clothing you got on the floor, on the ground over there. They put us in the shower with cold water, and some soap. And they start shaving us. And they were crazy-- screaming, and-- and beating, and-- and-- and mostly kapos, you know, German. Then they put us in a room like this-- 250 people sitting at one another. You couldn't fit over there, they beat you until you fit in. And-- they called us for food in the morning. They beat us-- they hit us when we ate, before we ate, after we ate. It was going on about three, four days. Then came the list, and we went up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all of you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Only people on the list, which was made before we left Plaszow, including the 50 families what Madritsch put on the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all the people that came from Plaszow to Gross-Rosen, did they all leave with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was special transport, which was assigned to Brunnlitz. The rest went to Mauthausen, Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: From-- from-- that went from Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Plaszow, that's right. And women to Bergen-Belsen, all over they spread. Because the camp had to be cleaned-- liquidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Plaszow had to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because the Russian was about, I would say, 80 miles from Krakow-- staying.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you-- when you left Gross-Rosen, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put us in cattle trains. We wind up in Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you just men? Just men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going when you got on this transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We finally, finally when we came close to Czechoslovakia, we saw the sign, Brunnlitz, at the railroad station. So we knew we were in Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that Schindler was in charge of Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew, because he organized the camp over there. He told the German authorities how he wants a-- how he wants to continue producing munitions for the Wehrmacht. So they gave him. Naturally, he got connections, so-- he wasn't there the day we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened the day you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got-- put us over there, and-- four deep. And the new chief of the camp-- his name was Leopold-- went through. And the young people-- there were a few people, what, eight years, 10 years, 13 years old, pulled them out with their parents and sent them to Auschwitz. And we went over there, with the-- they gave us straw. This was our mattresses. And-- later on, they  built something. And then we start building the factory. One-- we brought the big machines. This took-- then the women came.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you were there did the women  come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they came up three or four weeks later from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'd already built the factory at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were start building the factory. Yeah, you have to put together the big metal things, you know. And-- and it was a big place over there where all the factory was built-- cleaners, mechanics, electricians, you know, hooking up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they put me over there to-- there was  a big machine over there, which you pressed the pedal. The top came down, and you put over there some metal. And this would make a form like a-- like a-- for bullets, thing. That's  what I was doing all day, until I got tired of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took a hammer and put over there and broke the machine. It was a lot of noise , searching and looking. All the experts didn't know what happened. machine broke. So then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they put me in some other things, you know. And before you know, you know, that was already end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked you just going back to Plaszow, were there children in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were-- oh, they went-- they took them-- when they liquidated Plaszow, they took them all to Auschwitz, or to other camps. And they finished them off.

speaker: interviewer
text: With their parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow, the people from Brunnlitz-- the four or five fathers with their sons, went in to Auschwitz. And they survived. They are still alive. And the kid's parents-- I don't know, because in Plaszow it was-- Auschwitz was liberated in March or something-- or January, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow they survived. But in Plaszow, they took them away, you know, in groups. The whole group. I remember once-- one woman over there, was hiding about 10 children. You know where? In the-- in the latrine. Put him over there, and they stay over there until this was over. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And listen, if I had known it would take away, never have done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you got to Brunnlitz and then the women came? Did you think that you were going to see your wife again?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I saw her, I-- you know, because we were-- our problem was in Brunnlitz, food. That's what they gave us over there-- nothing. So what we did-- we went outside, which was a field. We dig out-- dug out over there beets, big ones, and we'd make fire and bake them. Then we were stealing potatoes from the magazine and bake them. Schindler once in a while gave us extra bread, and once in  a while some other things. But-- things were-- peas, bread in the morning. Soup lunchtime, which was water. And that's all. And the same thing in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember, if another three, four weeks, if the war was ended, I would be finished. I couldn't-- I lost so  much weight, I couldn't winds on three steps up. Did everybody feel the same way as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people got a whole more food. They went outside. They were a few Czechs. They brought you inside. They worked with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought some food. You know what I mean? But some, nobody was killed in Brunnlitz. Some people died, naturally dead. But no one was killed. Actually, another two, three weeks, there would be more-- be more dead from hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going like-- like animals, looking on the ground. We used to steal from dogs, food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what was everybody doing? You broke the machine that you were doing. And then what were other people doing?

words.text: And then what were other people

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was busy with something. The guy was polishing, this guy was cleaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything come out of the factories? Did you produce anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We produced. But it never came out. Nothing came out from the factory. And this was-- because Schindler knew, any day it was over. And the best proof is, when the war broke out-- end, he was in the camp with us. They opened the magazines, you know, with the German-- this was [INAUDIBLE], this was a part of Europe where the war ended. And there were a lot of trains, a lot of warehouses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took some material piece goods, some food, you know, he brought over there, and he gave us after the war. So my wife sew me pants, because we didn't have nothing, you know? And some food. Naturally, we got better food after the war ended. And that's--and the Czechs came over to help us. You know. Can I just ask you, when-- did you hear news from the outside world when you were in Brunnlitz about what was happening with the war that the Germans were losing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got the one guy over there who was the expert. We-- somehow he got hold of the toilet paper-- the newspaper in the toilet. He brought the pieces over there, and start reading over there, and figure out where are they-- where, what's what. And that's the way we know what's-- the end is coming. But no, we couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any dealings with Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one-- one bad thing, I mean. You see, when he took the 15 families from Madritsch, we came over there the first days. He got his people over there, you know, instead of working for him, they came to us. Hey, I know, you people from Madritsch got money with you, and this, we want you to contribute so we can buy more bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, how-- how can you have money, when we went through Gross-Rosen? They took everything-- I mean, the clothing, and this here. So they put us to carry 50 pounds sacks with cement to punish us. If this was Schindler's order, or somebody's, we don't know. Finally, after 15 days, they took us off. Schindler got one hobby. He used to go through the camp over there, in the worker shops, looking. He knew people from before. Oh, how you doing? This, this. He came to me with a strange face to him, because I was in the [NON-ENGLISH], where was his factory. He says, you come from where? I said, from Madritsch. He's got a hobby-- he threw butts of cigarettes on the floor, so he could pick up later. He got some tobacco.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he found out that I am from Madritsch, he took the butts, and with his shoes squeezed it so I couldn't pick it up. If this is the reason, because he figured out we come over, then we're going to give him some money or something. But he treated us, the 15 families, very bad. So this was my experience with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he find out I'm from Madritsch, he step on the butts so I couldn't pick it up. Besides this, I had no-- no business with him, because he's got his own people over there, you know? And then he's got this-- another-- SS man who took care of the camp. And-- Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Leopold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he good to the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he couldn't be good, because, no, he wouldn't be SS running the camp. You know what  I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me give you an example. When people were stealing potatoes, they were in the ground, you know, for winter. So he caught a few guys stealing potatoes. You know what he did? He took them and put them in the middle of the working place, put a big potato in his mouth, open. And he kept him for 24 hours like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Saliva was running over there. He was green and blue. That's what he did. That's what I saw. Then if, you know, he didn't like you, slap you in the face or something. And once in a while, we got some additional food from the Red Cross. They gave us tobacco.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Red Cross ever come into any of the camps you were in that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you couldn't do nothing. I'll give you an example. The  Red Cross came in Plaszow. And they were in preparation-- maybe you saw the movie-- Theresienstadt, how they prepared some Red Cross or some came. There were instructions. If we asked questions-- an example, Goeth used to stay behind the commission, and ask, how many breads are you getting?

speaker: interviewee
text: So Goeth, behind us, showed two fingers. Naturally, if you didn't say what he showed you, you'll be dead in a half hour after the commission leave. So this was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime we got cheeses of some kind from-- from the Red Cross-- powdered milk or something. But it helped, but it wasn't every day. And-- and Schindler, the same thing. Like I said, Schindler, I wouldn't say, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like he-- he was-- I wouldn't take away from him what he did, but why  he did-- to save himself, he needed people over there in Brunnlitz so they can testify. He put some shows over there so he could remember what he did. After the war, naturally, Schindler was supported by us-- contribution, money, incentives. They gave him $30,000 right away from the-- from the Joint.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Joint Distribution Committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right. He was a drunkard, a womanizer. He put it-- and in a few months, there was nothing left. Then they collected money again for him so he could go to Argentina. And they brought him over there-- I don't know-- the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point do you think that Schindler decided to change his attitudes and try and save the Jews and be good to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942, when he figured out how I got so much money. I got so many jewelry-- how to keep this, and stay-- because he'd be a millionaire after the war. The only thing-- he knew one thing, his life is in danger after the war, because of his Nazi background.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he figured, if I'm going to save the Jews, they are going to save me. That's what my thing is-- one hand watches another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say he was bad. But he didn't do-- I show you here even statements by more prominent people than I, who studied this. And he was a businessman. He did this for his own safety. Naturally, we gained. But I wouldn't put him over there in the-- in Jerusalem in the righteous thing, no. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because if I start digging, I find something about him, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, she was a nice woman-- a good woman. Let's see, my wife was in the camp in Brunnlitz. And because of lack of food and the food that they gave us, she got ulcers. And she was most of the time in the hospital over there. And one day, the doctor came and said, listen, if you're not going to find some milk for your wife, she's not going to last another 12 hours. I remember I went to her, and she gave me milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: She gave you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milk for my wife. She was good. She came and-- and talked to people. She gave them hiding, you know, from other things. Don't forget, we got German SS men who watched us all the time. So she was-- she was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she is rewarded now because of it. You know this? They bought her a home over there in Argentina. She lives over there. And she gets a pension, too. What happened in the last days of the war with the SS people that were living in the camp and with Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I give you example what happened last day-- the day before. The only one time I was hit by  a German was the day before the war ended. We were staying over there. And they were counting us. And there was a German with a sheet. And I didn't look at his face. Straight in the face, he slapped me. I was looking the next day for him. I mean, I hardly could walk, but I figured I have enough strength. But they all ran away during the night. All the Germans-- the only thing that was left is Schindler at the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that they were going to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know. They were normal-- everything. The counted us. And they put us to work in this here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did he slap you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't look in his face. When he called achtung, you know, thing, I didn't look at his face straight. It was the only one time I was hit by a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you respond?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't respond nothing. He's got a gun, I got nothing. So I just looked at his eyes over there. And the next day, he was gone with the rest of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this during the night that they left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, during the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you woke up the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, there were rumors that the war had ended. They didn't call us already to count. And Schindler-- I came, I remember-- my wife was in the hospital over there. I came to her, you know, and I says, you know, Annette? I think the war is over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't make on her and on me-- like nothing. It was over. So what? Because we were hungry. We were looking like dogs for food. So I says, yeah, I says, yeah-- and then-- when I-- again, then they called us over there, in the working place, and Schindler made that speech to us. That the war is over, and not to-- not to look after the Germans. I mean, they were taking orders. That's the reason they did it, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he put a tape-- Churchill made a speech in England. So he put over the speech in English. And he stayed with us. Then, after a few days, he got a hold of eight guys from camp that put a striped uniform on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he tell you he was nervous about what was going to happen to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't-- listen, remember one thing. He was a guy-- he couldn't-- hit him, he wouldn't react differently. He got over there a few guys who went, he took a truck, put all his things on the truck with all the jewelry, with all the thing, and his wife. And they smuggled him from Brunnlitz through the border to Czechoslovakia to Germany, so he would be safe. Because in-- the second day after the war ended, they came after him. And we wouldn't let them into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted to hang him up, because they knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: The local people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The local people. They knew who he is. And there was an underground, too, in the mountains over there that was supposed to be-- they were connected with us. I don't know how-- about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came after him. They said, oh, no, we protect him. And we took him out, and brought to Germany. Then-- and all the DP camps over there-- he was with them. They support him and connections for him. So who liberated your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened when the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were--we were looking for food. I ran out from the camp, and were looking for food for my wife or milk or something, because she was too weak, she couldn't stay on her feet. So I saw the columns going, going, going. And naturally, they start brainwashing us right away. We-- you are going to be OK. You join the party-- Communist Party. You'll be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, no, don't bother me. I've got enough on my-- of there to find something to eat. So they gave us a big can marmalade. So the guy who went with me grabbed this and says, listen, don't eat this. You'll get sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back to the camp. He was screaming from pain from the marmalade what they gave him. But later on, you know, it got better because the Czechs came. You know, there was some Czech who were collaborators. They needed us to protect them over there. So they came and put on the table everything-- butter, bread, meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't--you didn't feel that you wanted retribution against the Czechs who were collaborators?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't-- actually, we didn't have contact with them. After this, we found out that some few guys that over there to the Germans. They went to the apartments over there, start cleaning the apartments. One German-- and they spit at one German in the face. So he couldn't take it, and she shot himself. He committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who? The German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then they ran away too, the collaborators, from the-- You could see Germans working over there without shoes-- without this-- you know, prisoners. Because Brunnlitz was a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you after you were liberated. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I waited another week until my wife gets a little more strength. And I went back to Krakow. I figured out, maybe some of my family was alive. Because finally, I didn't know what happened to them, how they died, if they died. But nothing. I came after three days to Krakow, and stayed in Poland eight months and went back to a DP camp in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which DP camp were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankfurt am Main. Zeilsheim and Frankfurt, near Frankfurt am Main.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My job was buying some things and sell it to the Polish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] guards over there. They brought their stuff. I gave my stuff. And then I was, no, I was working to get out and go to America. So, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you leave for America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In May 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came with your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife and our child, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in-- wait a minute, wait a minute-- oh, yeah, 1946, in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to the States. And your daughter was born in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was born in Germany, in Frankfurt at the DP camp. I left Poland when she-- when my wife was six months pregnant. So we left and came to Frankfurt. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, when you got to America, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I start working. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I start working. I came Tuesday-- Thursday I was working in a department store, S. Klein on Union Square.
